items.correct: Features of the tax and benefit system that automatically cushion the economy without new legislation — e.g. unemployment benefits rise in slumps; tax revenues fall as incomes fall, supporting demand.
items.misconception: Automatic stabilisers are interest rate changes by the central bank.
items.random_wrong: Automatic stabilisers are special government emergency funds.
items.why: Stabilisers are fiscal (tax/benefit) and automatic (no decision needed) — distinct from monetary policy or discretionary stimulus. Students confuse them with central bank action or with one-off bailout funds.

items.q: How does raising interest rates reduce inflation?
items.correct: Higher rates make borrowing more expensive and saving more attractive, reducing consumption and investment — lowering aggregate demand and slowing price rises.
items.misconception: Higher interest rates directly force shops to lower their prices.
items.random_wrong: Higher interest rates make money more valuable so inflation falls automatically.
items.why: The transmission runs through aggregate demand, not direct price control. Higher rates dampen spending, which cools demand-pull inflation. Students miss the demand-side mechanism.

items.q: What is the difference between nominal and real values?
items.correct: Nominal values are measured in current money; real values are adjusted for inflation to allow meaningful comparison over time.
items.misconception: Nominal means estimated and real means accurate.
items.random_wrong: Nominal values are written and real values are spent.
items.why: Both nominal and real values are 'accurate' — they answer different questions. Real values strip out inflation to reveal actual purchasing power changes. Critical for comparing wages or GDP across years.

items.q: How many UN Sustainable Development Goals are there?
items.why: Correct answer: 17. “10” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The Brundtland definition of sustainability dates from:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1987. “1972” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Year the SDGs were adopted by the UN:
items.why: The real figure is 2015. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
